_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 1
questions.prompt: Which line makes the `random` module available under its own name?
choices: import random
from random import randint
include random
using random
questions.explanation: `import random` binds the module name. Its functions are then reached with a dot, such as `random.randint(1, 6)`.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 2
choices: When this file is run directly
Every time the file is imported
Only after an error
Before Python reads the file
questions.explanation: Python sets `__name__` to `"__main__"` for the file that starts the program. Imported modules receive their module names instead.

questions.title: Trace Modules & Imports
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Importing the module keeps the source of `sqrt` clear. The dot says that the function comes from math. The resulting output is '5.0'.

questions.title: Modules & Imports good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules & Imports?
choices: Group code by one clear purpose.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Group code by one clear purpose. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))

from math import *
print(sqrt(16))
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A wildcard import hides where names came from and can replace an existing name. Importing the module keeps that boundary visible.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Imported files can run code'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Statements at the top level run when Python imports the file. Place demo code and direct user input inside a main guard so importing the module does not start them.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Statements at the top level run when Python imports the file. Place demo code and direct user input inside a main guard so importing the module does not start them.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules & Imports?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Prefer imports that show where a name came from.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Prefer imports that show where a name came from. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules & Imports?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Avoid wildcard imports.
questions.explanation: Avoid wildcard imports. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules & Imports?
choices: Keep top-level work small.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Keep top-level work small. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules & Imports?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Put demos and command-line input behind a main guard.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Put demos and command-line input behind a main guard. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
